title: Managing Personal Energy Through the Annual Cycle
subtitle: Seasonality of activity. Winter reflection, spring start, summer expansion, autumn harvest
description: This checklist offers an alternative to the culture of 'constant productivity' that ignores natural rhythms and leads to burnout. You'll learn how to synchronize your life and work with the four seasons, using each period's energy for specific types of tasks. Winter for depth and planning, spring for launches and growth, summer for expansion and socialization, autumn for reaping rewards and taking stock. This is ancient wisdom confirmed by modern chronobiology.

items.title: Winter: The Season of Reflection, Recovery, and Strategy
items.source: Katherine May — Wintering: The Power of Rest and Retreat in Difficult Times
items.text: In nature, winter is when trees shed leaves, animals hibernate or slow their metabolism, and the ground rests under snow cover. The human body also experiences reduced energy due to shorter daylight hours and cold. Modern culture demands we be equally productive year-round, which contradicts biology. Winter is the ideal time for introverted, deep work: 1) Reflection on the past year (journaling, analyzing lessons). 2) Strategic planning for the coming year (not tactical steps, but vision and large goals). 3) Learning and reading (long evenings are made for books and courses). 4) Nervous system recovery (more sleep, fewer social obligations). 5) Working on the 'foundation': health, habits, relationships with close ones in a cozy home environment. Trying to 'storm the peaks' in January often leads to burnout by February. Wiser to use winter for accumulating strength and clarity.
items.tip: From the winter solstice (late December) through the end of January, consciously lower your expectations of yourself. Focus on 'winter' activities: reading by the fireplace/candlelight, long talks with loved ones, journaling, broad-stroke annual planning. Postpone ambitious starts until February-March.

items.title: Spring: Season of Launches, Growth, and Renewal
items.source: Andy Goldsworthy — Time (Seasonal Art and Observation)
items.text: In spring, nature awakens: daylight increases, vitamin D levels rise, energy grows. This is a biologically determined time for new beginnings, cleansing, and active growth. Spring energy is centrifugal, directed outward. What's optimal to do in spring: 1) Launch new projects and initiatives (start-up energy is maximal). 2) Declutter space and schedule (spring cleaning is not just a tradition, but synchronization with nature's renewal cycle). 3) Increase social activity after winter 'seclusion'. 4) Implement new habits (body is more plastic and ready for change). 5) Start outdoor physical activity. Spring is planting time. Don't expect immediate harvest, but actively sow seeds of future success: connections, projects, learning, habits.
items.tip: Create a 'spring planting list': 3-5 specific actions you will launch in March-April that will bear fruit by autumn. This could be starting a blog, enrolling in a course, beginning training, finding a mentor. Focus on action, not immediate results.

items.title: Summer: Season of Expansion, Socialization, and Visibility
items.source: Jaak Panksepp — Affective Neuroscience (Play and Seek Systems)
items.text: Summer is the peak of energy, light, and warmth. In pre-industrial times, this was a time of intensive work (planting, haymaking, berry picking) and simultaneously — holidays and community gatherings. For modern people, summer offers unique opportunities: 1) Expanding your network (conferences, festivals, summer terraces, informal communication). 2) Increasing professional visibility (less competition for attention in summer, many on vacation). 3) Travel that provides new perspectives and inspiration. 4) Intensive outdoor physical activity (swimming, cycling, hiking). 5) Creative experiments and a 'playful' state (the brain in warmth and abundant light is more creative). Important: summer expansion requires a good 'battery'. If you're drained from winter and spring without rest, summer can become a time of burnout. Balance activity with quality recovery.
items.tip: Schedule 2-3 'expansion' actions for summer that go beyond your usual routine: speaking at a conference, organizing a networking dinner, launching a collaboration, traveling to a new place with a professional or creative focus. Use the season's energy to push boundaries.

items.title: Autumn: Season of Harvest, Completion, and Integration
items.source: Lao Tzu — Tao Te Ching (The Cycle of Return)
items.text: In nature, autumn is a time of harvest, preparation for winter, and the beginning of slowing down. Energy becomes centripetal, directed inward. In traditional cultures, this was a time of thanksgiving, taking stock, and preserving provisions. In modern rhythms, autumn is ideal for: 1) Completing projects launched in spring, and 'harvesting' (bonuses, results, recognition). 2) Structuring and systematizing the year's accumulated experience and materials. 3) Financial planning and 'closing Gestalts' before year's end. 4) Strengthening close relationships before winter 'seclusion'. 5) Letting go of excess: not just things, but obligations, projects, relationships that bore no fruit. Autumn is a time for honest assessment: what worked, what didn't, and why. This knowledge becomes 'seeds' for the next spring.
items.tip: Conduct an 'autumn audit of the year': take goals set in January or spring and honestly assess progress. Which goals do you give up on (that's normal)? Which do you carry over to next year? Which do you complete in the remaining months? Write down the year's main lessons.

items.title: Micro-seasons: Monthly and Weekly Rhythms
items.source: Daniel Pink — When: The Scientific Secrets of Perfect Timing
items.text: Seasonality manifests not only in the annual cycle but also in monthly and weekly cycles. Daniel Pink's research shows: productivity and mood fluctuate throughout the week (Tuesday-Wednesday peak for analytics, Friday for creativity and relationships). For women, the menstrual cycle strongly influences energy and cognitive abilities: follicular phase (after menstruation) — peak energy, testosterone, great for starts and complex tasks; luteal phase (before menstruation) — time for completion, systematization, calmer work. Ignoring these micro-rhythms and demanding equal productivity daily is a path to disappointment and self-criticism. The ability to plan your week and month considering your individual rhythms (morning lark/night owl, cycle) is an advanced level of energy management.
items.tip: For two weeks, keep an 'energy diary': 3 times a day (morning, noon, evening) rate your energy level on a scale of 1 to 10. Note what tasks you're doing. Find patterns: which days and hours you're most productive for complex mental work, and which for routine or communication.

items.title: Seasonal Year Planning: The Quarterly Mapping Method
items.source: Tiago Forte — The PARA Method (Seasonal Adaptation)
items.text: Instead of a traditional annual plan that 'breaks' by February, use quarterly planning synchronized with the seasons. Break the year into 4 90-day quarters, each with its own 'theme' and focus. Q1 (Jan-Mar) = Winter-Spring: Reflection, planning, launching 1-2 key projects. Q2 (Apr-Jun) = Spring-Summer: Active growth, ramping up, expansion. Q3 (Jul-Sep) = Summer-Autumn: Peak visibility, collecting intermediate results, beginning of completion. Q4 (Oct-Dec) = Autumn-Winter: Project completion, taking stock, recovery, strategy. Within each quarter, set no more than 1-2 major goals. This approach is much more flexible and psychologically comfortable than trying to hold 10 goals in your head for the year.
items.tip: Take a calendar and mark out 4 quarters. For each quarter, define ONE main theme (e.g., 'Product Launch', 'Learning and Certification', 'Health Work', 'Book Writing'). All other tasks support this main theme or are postponed.

items.title: Winter and Summer Solstice: Anchor Points
items.source: John Matthews — The Winter Solstice: The Sacred Traditions of Christmas
items.text: Solstices and equinoxes are not just astronomical events, but powerful psychological anchors used across cultures for resetting and tuning. Winter Solstice (around Dec 21) — the longest night of the year, the point of maximum darkness and simultaneously — the turn towards light. Traditionally, a time for lighting fires, taking stock, and making wishes for the new cycle. Summer Solstice (around June 21) — peak of light, maximum outward energy. Spring and Autumn Equinoxes — balance points where day equals night, ideal for 'auditing' and course correction. Mindfully living these four days of the year as mini-transition rituals helps synchronize internal clocks with cosmic rhythms.
items.tip: Mark four key dates in your calendar: Winter Solstice (~Dec 21), Spring Equinox (~Mar 20), Summer Solstice (~Jun 21), Autumn Equinox (~Sep 22). On each of these days, set aside 30-60 minutes for reflection and tuning into the next season. Light a candle, write down thoughts.

items.title: Thermal and Light Hygiene Across Seasons
items.source: Andrew Huberman — Huberman Lab Podcast (Light and Temperature episodes)
items.text: Seasonal energy management is impossible without considering the two main Zeitgebers (rhythm drivers) for the body: light and temperature. Winter: maximize morning light (even on overcast days, go outside for 10-15 minutes without glasses), use daylight lamps (10,000 lux) upon waking, evenings — warm dim light and candles. Contrast temperatures (sauna/banya, cold shower) help 'awaken' thermoregulation. Summer: use morning light to set circadian rhythm, but avoid bright light late evening. Keep bedroom cool for quality sleep. Spring/Autumn: use transition periods for hardening (contrast shower, walks in any weather) to prepare the immune system for extreme seasons.
items.tip: Get a light therapy lamp (10,000 lux, no UV) and use it in winter for 20-30 minutes immediately after waking. This compensates for lack of natural light and maintains energy levels and mood.

items.title: Seasonal Eating as Fuel for Corresponding Energy
items.source: Paul Pitchford — Healing with Whole Foods: Asian Traditions and Modern Nutrition
items.text: Traditional Chinese medicine and Ayurveda have always emphasized the importance of seasonal eating. Summer: the body requires light, cooling, water-rich food (vegetables, fruits, greens). Winter: warming, dense, fatty food (root vegetables, stewed meat, soups, spices). Spring: cleansing, bitter greens helping the liver after heavy winter diet. Autumn: sweet root vegetables and pumpkins, preparing the body for cold. The modern availability of any product year-round disrupts this tuning, potentially contributing to seasonal affective disorder, winter lethargy, and summer overexcitation. Consciously choosing more 'seasonal' foods helps the body navigate the annual cycle more gently.
items.tip: Each season, go to a farmers market and buy what is currently grown in your region. Build your weekly menu around these foods. In winter: root vegetables, cabbages, apples. In summer: berries, greens, zucchini. Notice the difference in how you feel.

items.title: Emotional Seasons: Permission for 'Winter Blues' and 'Summer Euphoria'
items.source: Sheryl Paul — The Wisdom of Anxiety (Seasonal Emotional Cycles)
items.text: Modern psychology increasingly recognizes that emotional fluctuations throughout the year are not pathology but normal. Winter 'blues', decreased motivation, desire for solitude — these are not depression, but the psyche's natural response to decreased light and cold, an invitation to introspection. Summer euphoria, increased sociability — also part of the cycle. Problems begin when we resist these natural states, trying to be equally 'cheerful' all year, and start criticizing ourselves for it. Permitting yourself to feel what you feel in a given season, without labels of 'bad' or 'unproductive', reduces anxiety and frees up energy spent fighting yourself.
items.tip: In winter, when you feel low energy and a desire to hide, tell yourself: 'This is normal. It's the season of slowing down. I give myself permission for fewer tasks and more coziness.' In summer, when you feel a surge and desire to socialize, say: 'This is normal. It's the season of expansion. I'll use this energy for meetings and new experiences.'

quiz.q: What type of activity is optimal for the winter season in terms of energy management?
a: Launching new projects and active networking

quiz.q: Which season is biologically and psychologically optimal for launching new projects and beginnings?
What is the main 'theme' of the summer season in the context of the annual cycle?
What are the two main 'Zeitgebers' (rhythm drivers) for the body influencing seasonal energy?
Which planning method best synchronizes with the seasonal approach to energy?
a: Rigid annual plan with monthly breakdown
Quarterly planning with one main theme per season
Daily to-do lists without long-term planning
